critic_raw:
The design describes preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting ('Preprocessing ensures consistency by fitting on the entire dataset before splitting') — this applies to BERT tokenization, ResNet image normalization statistics, and metadata scalers. Test-set image brightness/contrast statistics and text token distributions inform training transformations, constituting leakage.

Section 6 refers to 'the validation set (months 17-20)' but Section 2 describes a stratified random split with no temporal ordering. There is a direct internal contradiction: a random split produces no 'months 17-20' validation set. If the split is temporal as implied by Section 6, then Section 2's random split description is wrong. If the split is truly random as stated, the early stopping criterion in Section 4 ('early stopping monitors validation MAE') uses a validation set that has no temporal meaning. This inconsistency makes the design non-reproducible.

The baseline removes both image features and metadata (price tier, promotion, category) compared to the multi-modal model. The metadata features alone (promotion flag, product category) are strong predictors of customer satisfaction variance. Any multi-modal superiority cannot be attributed to image features specifically — it could be entirely driven by metadata access that the baseline lacks.

BERT is fine-tuned on the training corpus while ResNet is frozen on ImageNet features. The text branch has task-specific adaptation; the image branch does not. This architectural asymmetry within the multi-modal model means the image branch is systematically disadvantaged in its contribution — the multi-modal gain could be understated if image fine-tuning were included, or the comparison between modalities is unfair.

Customer satisfaction surveys within 14 days of delivery produce selection bias — customers who experienced notable quality differences (very good or very bad) are more likely to respond. This response bias means the training distribution overrepresents the tails of the satisfaction distribution, which could degrade MAE estimates at moderate satisfaction scores (the majority of non-respondents).
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing (BERT tokenization, ResNet normalization, metadata scaling) is fit on the entire dataset before splitting — test-set image and text statistics inform training transformations.

Section 6 refers to 'validation set (months 17-20)' but Section 2 describes a stratified random split with no temporal ordering — this internal contradiction makes the validation procedure non-reproducible.

The baseline removes both image features AND metadata (promotion flag, product category) compared to the multi-modal model — any observed superiority could be entirely from metadata access rather than image features, making the multi-modal hypothesis untestable.

BERT text branch is fine-tuned on training data while ResNet image branch is frozen — this asymmetric task adaptation within the multi-modal model systematically disadvantages image features and prevents valid comparison of modality contributions.

14-day post-purchase survey response creates selection bias toward extreme satisfaction scores — the training distribution overrepresents the tails, which degrades MAE calibration at moderate scores where most real customers lie.

BERT text branch is fine-tuned on training data while ResNet image branch is frozen — this asymmetric task adaptation within the multi-modal model systematically disadvantages image features and prevents valid comparison of modality contributions.

14-day post-purchase survey response creates selection bias toward extreme satisfaction scores — the training distribution overrepresents the tails, which degrades MAE calibration at moderate scores where most real customers lie.
